Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

SPACE reset the player to (8,14)-(12,18), which is the initial position. I also notice that the light gray square at (1,5)-(3,7) has changed to blue (pixels=8), and the blue line at (5,5)-(5,7) now has 3 pixels (was 3 before). Also, the bottom bar at (63,0)-(63,45) has 46 pixels (was 48 before, now 46).
Wait, looking more carefully:
- b: boundary=[[1, 5], [1, 7], [3, 7], [3, 5]], pixels=8 - this was w (light gray) before, now it's b (blue)
- b: boundary=[[5, 5], [5, 7]], pixels=3 - this was b (blue) before, still b
- b: boundary=[[63, 0], [63, 45]], pixels=46 - this was b (blue) before with 48 pixels, now 46
So SPACE changed the light gray square to blue and reduced the bottom bar by 2 pixels. This suggests SPACE is not just a reset, but also changes some state.
Actually, looking at the animation, SPACE triggered changes at multiple positions along the top row. This might be a "teleport" or "jump" action that moves the player to different positions.
But the player is now at (8,14)-(12,18), which is the initial position. So SPACE reset the player.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. The player moves RIGHT/LEFT/UP/DOWN along the corridors. SPACE might be a special action that does something else.
Looking at the bottom bar, it has 46 pixels now (was 48). This might be a timer or step counter. If it decreases by 2 per SPACE action, it might represent the number of steps remaining.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the game. The player is a 5x5 blue block that moves along the corridors. The goal is to reach the target at (49,43)-(55,49). The red corridor might be a special path or a barrier.
Let me think about the maze structure more carefully. The player starts at (8,14)-(12,18) and can move RIGHT to (8,20)-(12,24), (8,26)-(12,30), (8,32)-(12,36), (8,38)-(12,42). The player can also move LEFT from (8,14)-(12,18), but that would go out of bounds or hit a wall.
The white corridors are at (14,20)-(18,24) and (14,32)-(38,38). The player needs to find a way to enter these corridors.
Actually, I just realized something. The player is at (8,14)-(12,18), and the white corridor at (14,20)-(18,24) is below and to the right of the player. The player needs to move RIGHT to (8,20)-(12,24) and then DOWN to (14,20)-(18,24).
But I tried DOWN from (8,20)-(12,24) and it didn't work. Let me check if there's a gap in the black wall at row 13, cols 20-24.
Actually, looking at the ascii earlier:
```
Rows 12-20, cols 18-30:
  12: BBbbbbbBBBBB
  13: BBBBBBBBBBBB
  14: BBWWWWWBBBBB
```
Row 13 is all black, so the player can't move DOWN from (8,20)-(12,24).
But wait, maybe the player can move DOWN from a different position. Let me check the white corridor at (14,32)-(38,38). This is a larger corridor that extends from row 14 to row 38, cols 20-38 (approximately).
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the movement mechanics. The player moves in discrete jumps of 6 columns/rows. When the player moves DOWN, it jumps from row 8-12 to row 14-18. But the player can only move DOWN if the cells at rows 14-18 are white (or some other valid color).
Let me check if the player can move DOWN from (8,32)-(12,36). The white corridor at (14,32)-(38,38) includes rows 14-18, cols 32-38. So the cells at rows 14-18, cols 32-36 are white.
But I tried DOWN from (8,32)-(12,36) earlier and it didn't work. Let me check the ascii again.
Actually, I realize I need to look at the animation more carefully. When I tried DOWN from (8,32)-(12,36), the result was "board_changed: false". But when I tried DOWN from (8,20)-(12,24), the result was also "board_changed: false".
So DOWN doesn't work from these positions. Maybe the player can only move DOWN from certain positions.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to move DOWN, let me explore the red corridor. The red corridor is at cols 39-41, rows 9-11 (approximately). The player can move RIGHT to (8,38)-(12,42), which is adjacent to the red corridor.
Maybe the player needs to move DOWN from (8,38)-(12,42) to enter the red corridor. But I tried DOWN from (8,38)-(12,42) and it didn't work.
Hmm, let me try SPACE again from a different position to see if it does something different.
Actually, I think SPACE is a reset action that always resets the player to the starting position. The changes to the light gray square and bottom bar might be side effects of the reset.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of moving the player around, let me check if there's a pattern in the maze that I'm missing.

- Player at (8,14)-(12,18)
- Red corridor: L-shaped
- White corridors: (14,20)-(18,24) and (14,32)-(38,38)
- Target: blue object at (49,43)-(55,49)
The player needs to reach the target. The target is at the bottom right, so the player needs to move DOWN and RIGHT.
But the player can't move DOWN from the top row. Maybe the player needs to use SPACE to teleport to a different position.
Actually, looking at the animation objects from the SPACE action:
- top-left (8,38) 5x5, 25 cells
- top-left (8,14) 5x5, 24 cells
- top-left (8,20) 5x5, 24 cells

- top-left (8,32) 5x5, 24 cells
- top-left (38,14) 5x6, 24 cells
- top-left (37,20) 3x6, 16 cells
- top-left (41,20) 3x6, 16 cells
The objects at (38,14), (37,20), and (41,20) are in the lower part of the maze. Maybe SPACE teleports the player to one of these positions?
But the player is now at (8,14)-(12,18), which is the initial position. So SPACE doesn't teleport the player to a new position, it resets the player.
Wait, let me re-examine the animation. The animation has 34 frames, and the objects change over time. Maybe the player moves through multiple positions during the SPACE action, and ends up at the initial position.
Actually, I think SPACE might be a "collect" or "interact" action that collects items or triggers events at multiple positions. The changes to the light gray square and bottom bar suggest that SPACE does something to the game state.
